items.correct: An initial injection of spending circulates as recipients spend portions, generating more income — total effect on GDP is larger than the initial injection.
items.misconception: The multiplier means government spending is always good because it multiplies wealth.
items.random_wrong: The multiplier is the rate at which interest compounds.
items.why: The multiplier is a mechanism, not a verdict. Whether to use it depends on conditions (slack in economy, marginal propensity to consume). Students confuse the descriptive concept with a policy prescription.

items.q: What did Adam Smith mean by the 'invisible hand'?
items.correct: That individuals pursuing their own self-interest in competitive markets can unintentionally produce socially beneficial outcomes through the price mechanism.
items.misconception: Adam Smith believed all selfish behaviour always benefits society.
items.random_wrong: The invisible hand refers to government regulating the economy secretly.
items.why: Smith was careful — the invisible hand works only under specific conditions (competition, no externalities, etc.). Students reduce him to a pure free-marketeer; his actual position was more nuanced.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.
The answer examiners want is: Opportunity cost. “Absolute output” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Ricardo. “Smith” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Export PI 110, import PI 100. Terms of trade =
items.why: The real figure is 110. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
